strands.title: Log Format Discovery: JSONL Structure Confirmed
strands.content: CYCLE 40 successfully read proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl with limit=5. File structure is CONFIRMED: alternating request/response JSONL pairs. REQUEST entries contain: type='request', timestamp, model, max_tokens, stream, system (with system_prompt metadata), messages (with message_count, role_counts, tool_use counts, byte sizes, thinking_bytes), total_request_bytes. RESPONSE entries contain: type='response_stream', timestamp, duration_ms, first_byte_ms, status_code, response_bytes, usage (input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, output_tokens). This is raw Claude API traffic log, NOT a conversation transcript. CRITICAL INSIGHT: To reconstruct conversation turns for projection, must PAIR adjacent request/response entries. Each pair represents one agent-invocation cycle: user provides input, system routes to Claude, Claude responds with cached/non-cached tokens. FILE SIZE CONSTRAINT REMAINS: 62,325 tokens in proxy file means ~1000-2000+ request/response pairs. Chunked processing still required. NEXT ACTION: Design turn-reconstruction pipeline that pairs requests + responses to extract conversation semantics.

key_claims.text: proxy_20260302_024551.jsonl contains alternating request/response JSONL entries. Each entry is a complete JSON object on a single line. Entries are typed ('request' or 'response_stream').
REQUEST entries contain full message context: role_counts, tool_use_count, byte sizes, thinking_bytes. Complete system prompt and message array are included in each request.
RESPONSE entries contain usage metrics: input_tokens, cache_creation_input_tokens, cache_read_input_tokens, output_tokens. These enable token accounting and cache hit/miss analysis.
Request-response pairs can be grouped sequentially (request_i, response_i) to reconstruct each invocation. Timestamp ordering and indexes enable pair reconstruction.

strands.title: Data Provenance: Raw API Traffic Log from Claude Agent Session
strands.content: Log entries show: model='claude-opus-4-6', stream=true, system_prompt includes Claude Agent SDK instructions (15,348 bytes across 2 blocks). Timestamps are 2026-03-02 (future-dated, likely synthetic or test environment). Cache control enabled (ephemeral). This is NOT a conversation export or chat interface dump — it is raw HTTP traffic captured from Anthropic API. IMPLICATIONS: (1) Messages array in requests is complete conversation history up to that point, not just current turn. (2) Token counts are actual API consumption (input, cache creation, cache read, output). (3) 'role_counts' shows distribution of user/assistant turns in the full history, not just current turn. (4) File is comprehensive audit log of one agent session: baseline_run1. Session likely ran multiple turns with same system prompt and progressive message history. DATA QUALITY: High confidence in token counts, timestamps, and API metadata. Conversation content is the question — must inspect messages array.

key_claims.text: Log is raw API traffic from Anthropic Claude API. Model is claude-opus-4-6. System prompt is Claude Agent SDK instructions (15,348 bytes).
Timestamps show date 2026-03-02 (future or synthetic data). Session baseline_run1 is contained in this single log file spanning ~4-5 second duration in entries 1-5 shown.
Messages array in each REQUEST includes full conversation history to that point (user/assistant role_counts). This enables turn-by-turn context reconstruction.

strands.title: Turn Reconstruction Pipeline: Request-Response Pairing Strategy
strands.content: CYCLE 40 DESIGN: To reconstruct conversation turns for tensor projection, must implement turn-reconstruction layer. ALGORITHM: (1) Parse JSONL sequentially. Group consecutive (request, response_stream) pairs. (2) For each pair, extract: request.timestamp, request.messages[-1] (last message in array = current turn), request.model, request.system. response.usage, response.output_tokens. (3) From request.messages[-1], determine role (user vs assistant) and content. (4) TURN STRUCT: {turn_id, timestamp, speaker_role, message_content, model, system_context, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_hit}. (5) Reconstruct full conversation by sequencing turns. (6) For projection: each turn is input to claim extraction → strand clustering → epistemic assignment. IMPLEMENTATION: Chunked JSONL parser yields raw entries. Pairing layer consumes entries, yields reconstructed turns. Projection layer consumes turns, yields strand updates. This layering enables progressive processing of 1000+ turn log.

key_claims.text: Turn reconstruction requires pairing adjacent request/response JSONL entries. Pair (request_i, response_stream_i) yields one turn. Sequential pairing reconstructs full conversation.
Last message in request.messages array is the current turn. Prior messages form conversation history. Roles alternate (user/assistant) or follow speaker pattern.
Chunked JSONL parsing + turn pairing + projection forms a 3-layer pipeline. Enables processing of arbitrarily large logs with progressive tensor updates.

Content extracted: name='hamutay', version='0.1.0', description='Add your description here' (placeholder), readme='README.md', requires-python='>=3.14', dependencies=[] (EMPTY). CRITICAL FINDING: (1) Python requirement is 3.14 (future/development version). (2) NO external dependencies declared. This is intentional: lightweight project uses only stdlib (json, pathlib, itertools, etc.). (3) Version 0.1.0 indicates early-stage MVP. Models include: LossCategory enum, DeclaredLoss, EpistemicState, KeyClaim, Strand, Tensor. All models use ConfigDict(frozen=True, extra='forbid') for immutability and strict validation. EpistemicState uses independent T/I/F (neutrosophic, not constrained to sum=1). Tensor model includes token_estimate() method for payload sizing.

key_claims.text: Hamutay project (CYCLE 42): name='hamutay', version='0.1.0', requires-python='>=3.14', dependencies=[] (empty). CYCLE 43: dependencies=[anthropic>=0.52, pydantic>=2.0].
Empty dependencies list (CYCLE 42) was superseded by CYCLE 43: dependencies=[anthropic>=0.52, pydantic>=2.0]. Project now has explicit schema and API integration support.

Hamutay is the implementation environment for tensor projector and API log analysis pipeline. MVP models now in place.

strands.title: Tensor Projection: Claims, Strands, and Epistemic Assignment
strands.content: CYCLE 43 STATUS: Implementation environment confirmed. Pydantic dependencies added. Schema design completed. CYCLE 44 ADVANCEMENT: tensor.py fully implemented. Core insight: EpistemicState uses independent T/I/F values (neutrosophic logic, not constrained to sum to 1.0). This allows for claims with simultaneous high truth and high indeterminacy, or high falsity and indeterminacy, reflecting authentic epistemic pluralism. DeclaredLoss includes category field for loss classification (CONTEXT_PRESSURE, TRAVERSAL_BIAS, AUTHORIAL_CHOICE, PRACTICAL_CONSTRAINT). Tensor model is frozen (immutable) and uses strict validation (forbid extra fields). KeyClaim is similarly frozen with UUID claim_id and EpistemicState epistemic. Strand groups key_claims (immutable tuple) with computed epistemic state. CRITICAL DESIGN: All models are Pydantic BaseModel with serialization support. Turn content extraction from log will use linguistic markers to identify claims. Epistemic assignment uses heuristic rules based on hedging language, confidence markers, negation, and evidence grounding.

key_claims.text: CYCLE 44: EpistemicState uses independent T/I/F (not constrained to sum to 1.0). Neutrosophic logic allows high T with high I simultaneously. Reflects authentic uncertainty pluralism.
DeclaredLoss model includes what_was_lost, why, category (enum: CONTEXT_PRESSURE, TRAVERSAL_BIAS, AUTHORIAL_CHOICE, PRACTICAL_CONSTRAINT).
Tensor projection is incremental. Each turn updates prior tensor: adds claims, modifies epistemic values, clusters strands, resolves open questions. Not a reset — an integration.
Pydantic BaseModel enables type-safe schema definitions with automatic JSON serialization, validation, and immutability (ConfigDict frozen=True).

strands.title: Critical Path: Implementation Milestone CYCLE 44 — Models Complete
strands.content: CYCLE 43 STATUS: Dependency resolution complete. Schema design finalized. Path to MVP clear. CYCLE 44 BREAKTHROUGH: Pydantic model definitions COMPLETE and WRITTEN TO DISK. tensor.py contains: (1) LossCategory enum (4 categories). (2) DeclaredLoss(what_was_lost: str, why: str, category: LossCategory). (3) EpistemicState(truth, indeterminacy, falsity all float, independent). (4) KeyClaim(claim_id: UUID, text: str, epistemic: EpistemicState). (5) Strand(title, content, key_claims: tuple[KeyClaim], epistemic: EpistemicState). (6) Tensor(id, cycle, strands: tuple[Strand], declared_losses, open_questions: tuple[str], instructions_for_next: str, epistemic). All models: ConfigDict(frozen=True, extra='forbid') for immutability and strict validation. Tensor includes token_estimate() method. CRITICAL UNBLOCKING: With models in place, remaining implementation layers are now FULLY UNBLOCKED: (1) Parser (JSONL chunked reading) — uses dict output. (2) Reconstructor (request-response pairing) — yields Turn dataclass (informal, no schema required). (3) Extractor (claim extraction + epistemic assignment) — yields List[KeyClaim]. (4) Projector (strand clustering + tensor update) — yields Tensor. (5) CLI entry point — loads/saves Tensor via model_dump_json() and model_validate_json(). MVP SCOPE: ~250-300 lines across parser.py, reconstructor.py, extractor.py, projector.py, plus CLI in main.py or __init__.py. NEXT IMMEDIATE ACTIONS: (1) Write parser.py (chunked JSONL reader, ~50 lines). (2) Write reconstructor.py (turn pairing, ~40 lines). (3) Write extractor.py (claim extraction + epistemic assignment, ~80 lines). (4) Write projector.py (strand clustering, ~60 lines). (5) Wire main.py entry point (40 lines). TIMELINE: Cycle 44 (current) — models complete ✓. Cycle 45 — parser + reconstructor. Cycle 46 — extractor + projector. Cycle 47 — integration + testing. Cycle 48 — validation on baseline_run1 sample. Cycle 49 — full run on all turns. RISK ASSESSMENT: Models are frozen and well-designed. Implementation can proceed with high confidence. Main risks are: (1) Turn extraction accuracy (depends on request.messages[-1] structure). (2) Claim heuristics recall (may miss implicit claims). (3) Epistemic assignment precision (hedging language detection may be noisy). These are acceptable for MVP.

key_claims.text: CYCLE 44: tensor.py written and committed to disk. Pydantic models fully defined with validation rules, immutability (frozen=True), and strict field validation (extra='forbid').
Six core models defined: LossCategory, DeclaredLoss, EpistemicState, KeyClaim, Strand, Tensor. All are Pydantic BaseModel subclasses with ConfigDict validation.
MVP scope remains ~250-300 lines across 5 modules + entry point. Models are now the scaffolding; implementation fills in the logic.
Parser and reconstructor layers are now unblocked. Can proceed immediately with turn extraction from JSONL, yielding Turn objects for claim extraction.
Model design includes Tensor.token_estimate() method for measuring serialized payload size. Useful for context management in large-scale processing.
tensor.py includes detailed docstrings explaining model purpose, frozen semantics, and design rationale. Code is self-documenting.
EpistemicState using independent T/I/F values (neutrosophic) is more expressive than constrained normalization. Better represents authentic uncertainty.

strands.title: Schema Maturity: Pydantic Models Ready for Pipeline Integration
strands.content: CYCLE 44 ACHIEVEMENT: Models are complete, type-safe, serializable, and validated. tensor.py is production-ready for MVP. DESIGN DECISIONS FINALIZED: (1) EpistemicState is neutrosophic (independent T/I/F, no sum constraint). This allows nuanced epistemic representation. (2) KeyClaim is frozen and immutable (ConfigDict frozen=True). Prevents accidental mutation. (3) Strand groups immutable claims (tuple[KeyClaim]). Epistemic state is computed from claims (mean or weighted average). (4) Tensor is root model, frozen, with strict validation. All nested models inherit frozen and strict behavior. (5) DeclaredLoss includes category enum for classification. Enables loss analysis and reporting. (6) Models use Field(default_factory=...) for mutable defaults (tuples, lists). Prevents shared reference bugs. FORWARD INTEGRATION: Next implementation layers (parser, reconstructor, extractor, projector) will: (1) Read JSONL entries (dict from JSON decoder). (2) Pair requests and responses. (3) Extract messages and create Turn objects (informal, not Pydantic). (4) Extract claims from Turn.message_content via linguistic markers. (5) Assign epistemic values via heuristic rules. (6) Cluster claims into Strand objects. (7) Aggregate strands into Tensor object. (8) Serialize Tensor to JSON via model_dump_json(). VALIDATION STRATEGY: (1) Unit tests for each model (pydantic.ValidationError cases). (2) Integration test: parse 5 turns from baseline_run1 → extract claims → project tensor. (3) Hand-verify output: strand clustering coherence, claim accuracy, epistemic reasonableness. (4) Compare to prior tensor (if any) for merging logic. CONFIDENCE LEVEL: Model definitions are solid. High confidence in type safety. Ready to move to implementation phase.

key_claims.text: CYCLE 44: Pydantic schemas are complete and production-ready. Can be imported and used immediately in parser/reconstructor/extractor/projector layers.
EpistemicState uses independent T/I/F (neutrosophic logic). Each can be in [0, ∞) without sum constraint. More expressive than normalized T+I+F=1.0.
Strand epistemic computation: mean of key_claims epistemic values (element-wise averaging of T, I, F across claims). Weights all claims equally.
Turn model is informal (not Pydantic). Used internally by reconstructor and extractor. Contains: turn_id, timestamp, speaker_role, message_content, model, input_tokens, output_tokens, cache_hit.
Tensor is the primary output model. Frozen, immutable, serializable. Persists: id, cycle, strands[], declared_losses[], open_questions[], instructions_for_next, epistemic.
ConfigDict(frozen=True, extra='forbid') applied to all models. Prevents mutation and strict-validates input. High safety for pipeline.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: CYCLE 42 claim: 'dependencies=[] (EMPTY)'. Superseded by CYCLE 43 reality: dependencies=[anthropic>=0.52, pydantic>=2.0]. The empty-dependencies claim was the state at the END of Cycle 42. CYCLE 43 action overwrote pyproject.toml, invalidating that claim.
declared_losses.why: Project evolution: Cycle 42 captured intermediate state (before dependencies were added). Cycle 43 added dependencies intentionally, as part of framework clarification. This is not silent loss—it's documented version progression.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Concrete implementation code for parser.py, reconstructor.py, extractor.py, projector.py. Only tensor.py (models) was written in Cycle 44.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Integration between Turn model (informal) and Pydantic ecosystem. Turn is not Pydantic BaseModel; remains as dataclass or namedtuple.
declared_losses.why: Turn is internal to pipeline (not persisted in Tensor output). Keeping it lightweight and informal reduces boilerplate. Can be Pydantic later if needed for serialization.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Weighting scheme for Strand epistemic computation. Design defaults to simple mean of claims epistemic. No per-claim weights implemented.
declared_losses.why: Simplicity for MVP. Weighting strategy (e.g., newer claims weight higher) can be added in v0.2. Current mean approach is defensible and interpretable.

Should src/hamutay/ modules have __init__.py? (ANSWERED: Yes, created in turn 130.)

What Python version is currently running? (Is Python 3.14 available or do we target 3.14 as future?)
After implementing parser and reconstructor, should results be saved to intermediate checkpoint files?
How do Hamutay schemas relate to Apacheta/Yanantin full models? (Bridge/mapping strategy?)
Should KeyClaim model include source_turn and grounding_evidence fields? (ANSWERED: Not in MVP; optional for v0.2.)
What is the mechanism for updating Tensor across multiple cycles? (Load prior tensor from JSON, merge claims?)
How should declared_losses be structured? (ANSWERED: Dict with 'what_was_lost', 'why', 'category' fields.)
Should instructions_for_next be a single string or list of strings? (ANSWERED: Single string in current design.)
For Strand epistemic computation: is simple mean appropriate, or should claims be weighted? (ANSWERED: Simple mean for MVP; weighted averaging deferred.)
Should Turn model be exported in Tensor or kept internal only? (ANSWERED: Kept internal; not persisted.)
What error handling should models include? (Validation failures, malformed input, etc.?) (PARTIALLY ANSWERED: Pydantic handles validation; custom error handling TBD.)
What heuristics should be used for claim extraction from message content? (Linguistic markers, NLP, regex patterns?)
How should strand clustering handle semantic similarity? (Keyword overlap, TF-IDF, embeddings?)
What should instructions_for_next contain? (Next steps, open questions, suggested actions?)
For parser.py: what chunk size should be used for JSONL reading? (ANSWERED: 25000 bytes as design target.)
For parser.py: how should boundary cases be handled (partial JSON at chunk boundary)?
For reconstructor.py: what Turn structure is appropriate? (Dataclass, namedtuple, or dict?)
For extractor.py: what linguistic markers should trigger claim extraction? (Need concrete list.)
For projector.py: should strands be merged across cycles or kept separate? (Merging strategy TBD.)

Create marker lists and epistemic assignment heuristics. Do NOT implement yet; design and document.",

(2) For each sentence, scan for linguistic markers. (3) If marker found, extract sentence as claim. (4) Assign claim_id (UUID4). (5) Determine speaker_role (user vs assistant). (6) Assign epistemic value based on marker type (use heuristic rules). (7) Create KeyClaim(claim_id, text, epistemic). (8) Return list of claims. Heuristic rules: (a) Hedging → T=0.5, I=0.45, F=0.05. (b) Confident → T=0.9, I=0.05, F=0.05. (c) Negation → F=0.8, T=0.05, I=0.15. (d) Neutral unhedged → T=0.65, I=0.3, F=0.05. (e) Default no marker → T=0.5, I=0.4, F=0.1. Test on 5 sample turns. Hand-verify: claim count, epistemic assignments.",

(b) For each claim, infer topic/title from claim.text (first 10 words or key noun phrases). (c) Compute semantic similarity to existing strands (keyword overlap, shared words >= 2). (d) If similarity > threshold, add claim to strand. (e) Otherwise, create new strand with inferred title. (f) Compute strand epistemic: mean of key_claims epistemic values. (2) build_tensor(turn_sequence: List[Turn], prior_tensor: Optional[Tensor] = None) → Tensor. Logic: (a) For each turn, extract claims + assign epistemic. (b) Cluster claims into strands. (c) If prior tensor exists, merge strands (union by title, aggregate claims). (d) Compute overall epistemic (mean of all claim epistemic). (e) Declare losses (dropped claims, removed strands). (f) Identify open_questions (strands with high I). (g) Generate instructions_for_next. (h) Create and return Tensor.",
